topic: Internal course: Investigative Study of a Religious Practice
subject: religious-studies

items.q: How does Reform Judaism differ from Orthodox Judaism?
items.correct: Reform Judaism treats Jewish law (halakhah) as evolving and not strictly binding, embraces modernity, and grants gender equality; Orthodox Judaism holds halakhah as divinely given and binding in detail, with traditional practice maintained.
items.misconception: Reform Jews don't believe in God and Orthodox Jews do.
items.random_wrong: Orthodox Judaism is a different religion from Reform Judaism.
items.why: Both share belief in one God and Jewish identity. The difference is about authority and adaptability of law and practice, not the existence of belief. Students collapse this into a believer/non-believer split.

items.q: What is the difference between act and rule utilitarianism?
items.correct: Act utilitarianism judges each individual action by its consequences; rule utilitarianism follows rules that, if generally adopted, would produce the most good — sacrificing case-by-case calculation for predictability and fairness.
items.misconception: Act and rule utilitarianism are basically the same approach.
items.random_wrong: Act utilitarianism is about action movies and rule utilitarianism is about laws.
items.why: The distinction matters for famous counterexamples (e.g. punishing the innocent). Act utilitarianism might endorse it if outcomes are better; rule utilitarianism rejects such practices because the rule would be harmful overall.

items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.q: Whose 1902 Gifford Lectures examined mystical experience?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: William James. “Rudolf Otto” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which 16th-century Spanish mystic wrote Interior Castle?
items.why: It's Teresa of Avila. “John of the Cross” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Otto publish Das Heilige?
items.why: The real figure is 1917 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
